current_fact.fact_text: On March 13, 1989, Space Shuttle Discovery launched on mission STS-29 to deploy the TDRS-4 communications satellite, a key node in NASA’s Tracking and Data Relay Satellite network. This invisible infrastructure quietly transformed space operations, enabling near‑continuous contact with spacecraft instead of brief ground‑station windows.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

current_fact.match_reason: A mission that upgrades communication links matches a format that introduces a new transmission channel for Sedna FM, both quietly reshaping how signals flow.

queue.fact_text: In 1930, Lowell Observatory announced the discovery of Pluto, a tiny, distant world first spotted on photographic plates by 23‑year‑old Clyde Tombaugh. For decades it was hailed as the ninth planet, long before its reclassification as the most famous of the dwarf planets.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: A "cosmic" dance selection is a playful companion to the discovery of a distant world at the edge of the Solar System.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1781, William Herschel noticed what he thought was a comet—only to discover the first new planet found in modern times: Uranus. Its strange, tilted rotation and pale blue hue revealed that the Solar System was stranger and larger than previously imagined.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: The discovery of an icy, remote planet pairs with an episode set in cold Arctic waters, both exploring beautiful, distant blue worlds.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1845, Felix Mendelssohn’s Violin Concerto in E minor was premiered in Leipzig with his friend Ferdinand David as soloist. Now one of the most beloved concertos in the violin repertoire, it was once a daringly modern piece that blurred the lines between soloist and orchestra.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

queue.match_reason: A concerto that became a timeless classic aligns with an episode celebrating music that forms a 'timeless legacy' across generations.

queue.fact_text: In 2003, a study in the journal Nature identified the Ciampate del Diavolo site in Italy as containing fossilized hominid footprints about 350,000 years old. The prints, once woven into local legends about the Devil, turned out to be some of the oldest known traces of ancient humans calmly walking near an active volcano.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Ancient footprints reinterpreted through science mirror an episode about how learning reshapes what we thought we knew, turning myth into understanding.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1888, the volcanic island of Ritter in what is now Papua New Guinea catastrophically collapsed during an eruption, sending massive landslides into the sea. The resulting tsunamis raced outward and killed up to 3,000 people on nearby coasts, a grim example of how islands can literally fall into the ocean.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A violent planetary event is juxtaposed with 'Slow Planet', inviting reflection on Earth's raw power and the need for humble, slower rhythms in the face of it.

queue.fact_text: In 1992, the Mw 6.6 Erzincan earthquake struck eastern Turkey with a maximum Mercalli intensity of VIII, collapsing buildings and reshaping the landscape in seconds. It reminded seismologists that even well‑known fault systems can still deliver devastating surprises.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: An earthquake is literally a sudden shock from the Earth, making an 'Electric Shock' themed, high-energy rock set a fitting sonic echo of sudden seismic jolts.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1993, the "Storm of the Century" swept across the eastern United States, a colossal nor’easter that dropped feet of snow from Alabama to Maine. It packed hurricane‑force winds, spawned tornadoes, and showed how a single storm system can blanket half a continent.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: An episode centered on storms, rain, and weathering change is a direct emotional match for one of the most dramatic storms in modern U.S. history.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1940, the Winter War between Finland and the Soviet Union formally ended with the Moscow Peace Treaty. Despite being heavily outnumbered and outgunned, Finnish forces had held off the Red Army for months in brutal sub‑zero conditions, turning frozen forests into battlefields.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

queue.match_reason: A war fought through harsh northern winters and shifting borders pairs with an episode about northern skies, seasons, and finding what grounds you through change.

queue.fact_text: In 1815, while Napoleon had escaped from exile on Elba and marched back into France, the diplomats at the Congress of Vienna declared him an outlaw. In a single decision, one of Europe’s most powerful rulers was rebranded as a fugitive to be hunted down by the coalition powers.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A political order uniting to brand a former emperor an 'outlaw' resonates with an episode about power, resistance, and challenging political authority through music.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1323, Sultan Ghiyath al‑Din Tughluq sent an army led by his son Muhammad bin Tughluq to lay siege to the fortified city of Warangal after its ruler refused tribute. After eight months of resistance, Warangal finally fell, marking a decisive expansion of the Delhi Sultanate into the Deccan.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: A long siege and imperial expansion echo themes of endurance, contested lands, and resistance that run through Tuareg desert music.

queue.fact_text: In 1811, during the Napoleonic Wars, a smaller British squadron defeated a combined French and Italian fleet off the Adriatic island of Vis at the Battle of Lissa. The engagement became a textbook example of aggressive maneuvering and how superior tactics at sea could overturn numerical disadvantage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A historic naval battle off an island lines up neatly with an episode literally recorded at sea, both telling very different stories of life on the water.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1591, at the Battle of Tondibi in Mali, a Moroccan army led by the eunuch general Judar Pasha defeated the vast but poorly armed forces of the Songhai Empire. Gunpowder weapons and cannon shattered one of West Africa’s greatest empires, changing the balance of power along the Sahara’s trade routes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A pivotal West African battle and the reshaping of regional power resonate with an episode spotlighting modern West African musical revolutions and identity.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1697, the city of Nojpetén, capital of the last independent Maya kingdom, fell to Spanish conquistadors. Its capture marked the final step in the centuries‑long Spanish conquest of Guatemala and the formal end of unconquered Maya polities.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: The fall of the last independent Maya capital to Spanish forces connects with an episode that critically explores Spanish cultural history through Flamenco.

queue.fact_text: In 1957, Cuban student revolutionaries stormed the presidential palace in Havana in a bold attempt to assassinate dictator Fulgencio Batista. The operation failed, but it signaled the growing willingness of young Cubans to risk everything to challenge an entrenched regime.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

queue.match_reason: A student-led attack against a Caribbean dictator pairs with an episode exploring rebellious, politically charged Latin urban music born in the same broader region.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1567, the Battle of Oosterweel near Antwerp is traditionally regarded as the beginning of the Eighty Years’ War. A small Calvinist rebel force was crushed by Spanish troops, but the clash ignited a long struggle that would eventually lead to the independence of the Dutch Republic.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

queue.match_reason: An industrial-age Northern city soundtrack underscores an early modern rebellion that helped create a new state, linking themes of working people, independence, and common life.

queue.fact_text: On March 13 in the year 222, the Roman emperor Elagabalus was murdered alongside his mother by members of the Praetorian Guard. The teenage ruler—known for religious eccentricity and breaking social norms—was replaced by his 14‑year‑old cousin Severus Alexander, in a bloody reset of imperial image.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: An emperor notorious for radical self-presentation and shifting identities aligns with an episode about alter-egos and reinventing who you can be.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1964, Kitty Genovese was murdered in New York City, a crime that later inspired influential—if partly mythologized—research into the bystander effect. The story sparked decades of debate on how urban anonymity, perception, and social cues shape whether people step in to help.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A case that led psychologists to study human behavior in crowds connects with an introspective episode about how modern technology shapes our attention, empathy, and connection.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1997, the Missionaries of Charity chose Sister Nirmala Joshi to succeed Mother Teresa as their leader. A quiet, scholarly nun, she inherited a global network of homes for the poor and continued the order’s work after its charismatic founder’s death.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

queue.match_reason: A succession between two prominent women in a mission of service pairs with an episode celebrating powerful female voices and the different ways women lead and inspire.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1969, the Apollo Lunar Module—tested in space by Apollo 9—proved that a delicate, insect‑like craft could detach from a command ship, fly independently, and dock again. This ungainly vehicle, designed to work only in vacuum, would soon carry humans to the Moon’s surface.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A spacecraft built purely for travel in deep space matches an episode framed as a 'Destination' voyage through new musical territories and cosmic-tinged sounds.

queue.fact_text: On March 13, 1888’s Ritter Island collapse and tsunami also provided one of the earliest detailed modern records of how volcanic islands can catastrophically fail. Later geologists would use this event as a natural laboratory for understanding tsunami‑generating landslides.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A scientifically important tsunami event pairs with an episode themed around water, immersion, and moving with powerful currents rather than resisting them.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 2003, when scientists dated the Ciampate del Diavolo footprints to 350,000 years ago, it became clear that early humans had walked calmly near an erupting volcano, leaving a frozen moment of everyday courage. The site turned a local legend of "Devil’s footprints" into a tangible chapter of human evolution.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: Ancient footprints preserved in volcanic ash are imperfect, accidental records of life—mirroring an episode that celebrates happy accidents, rough edges, and the beauty of imperfection.

queue.fact_text: Also on March 13, 1969, when Apollo 9 splashed down, it marked not just a successful mission but an early victory in learning how humans and machines could cooperate in the void. Every safe return of a test crew quietly paved the way for moonwalks that would capture the world’s imagination.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: Apollo 9’s safe return was a 'where it all began' moment for lunar exploration, just as Sedna FM’s first episode was the launchpad for everything that followed on this station.

published.fact_text: In 1969, Apollo 9 returned safely to Earth after the first full test of the Lunar Module in orbit, proving that humans could undock, fly, and redock the spacecraft that would later land on the Moon. This “dress rehearsal in space” quietly solved dozens of engineering unknowns before the Apollo 11 landing was even attempted.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A spaceflight milestone pairs naturally with an episode themed around 'space' and mindset shifts, echoing how Apollo 9 opened creative room for the rest of the Moon program.

published.fact_text: On March 13, 1989, Space Shuttle Discovery launched on mission STS-29 to deploy the TDRS-4 communications satellite, a key node in NASA’s Tracking and Data Relay Satellite network. This invisible infrastructure quietly transformed space operations, enabling near‑continuous contact with spacecraft instead of brief ground‑station windows.
